name: Hearthbread
description: Dense, slightly sweet bread baked low and slow. The kind of food that keeps a person upright through a hard day. Not exciting. Deeply reliable.

name: Iron Stew
description: A long-cooked stew that takes most of an afternoon and rewards the patience. The ironroot breaks down into something almost melting. It fills the body like ballast.

name: Wanderer's Supper
description: A light meal built for people who eat on the move. The figs and berries keep well, the whole thing comes together quickly, and it leaves no heaviness behind.

name: Stormcap Sauté
description: Wild mushrooms cooked fast and hot with a handful of pale caps and a few heartflame peppers. Something about the combination produces a particular sharpness of movement.

name: Contemplative Tea
description: A simple infusion brewed from sunpetal herb and a few moonpetal leaves. Pale gold in the cup. Clears the mind like opening a window.

name: Moonpetal Tisane
description: A stronger preparation — three full measures of moonpetal with ironbark resin to fix the effect. It requires patience to brew and smells like a forest at midnight. The acuity it produces is complete and unnerving.

name: Ember Porridge
description: Hearthgrain cooked long with a careful hand of heartflame peppers — enough to warm, not enough to burn. Sits in the belly like a coal. Good for cold mornings and cold work.

name: Heartflame Broth
description: A deep, dark broth that takes skill to make without making it undrinkable. The ironroot provides body, the hearthgrain thickens it, and the peppers do what they always do. Keeps a person warm in conditions that have no business being survived.

name: Ranger's Fare
description: Cooked down over a small fire with the minimum of fuss. Ironroot for endurance, pale caps for what they add that no one quite explains. What rangers carry when they travel long and fight hard at the end of it. It does not taste like much. It works.

name: Bilberry Tea
description: Astringent, medicinal, and deeply unpleasant to drink. The bitterness is entirely the point. Something in bilberry cuts through fog and confusion and the particular weight that settles on a person who has been too long in unhealthy places. Tastes terrible. Works.

name: Restorative Broth
description: A plain broth, long-simmered. Ironroot for body, sunpetal herb for something the healers call 'knitting.' Not remarkable to eat. Remarkable for what it does afterward — tissue repairs faster, ache recedes, the body decides to continue.

name: Athelas Infusion
description: A preparation of moonpetal steeped with ironbark resin and something rarer still. Raw athelas is a weed. In the right hands, prepared the right way, it becomes something else entirely — the last line between a grievous wound and what comes after. It does not always work. When it does, it is extraordinary.
